claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 16 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 5 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in approved education
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 16 and in approved education; Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 16 and in approved education
Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority

Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you find out if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

Let's start: **How many children are you claiming Child Benefit for, and what are their names and ages?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit for my two children. I have Alex, who's 16, and Blake, who's 5 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! It's lovely to meet you. Let me ask a few more questions to assess your eligibility for both Alex (16) and Blake (5).

**Are both Alex and Blake living with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, actually Blake lives with me, but Alex doesn't live at home - Alex lives elsewhere.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. Since Alex lives elsewhere, I need to understand that situation a bit more.

**Could you tell me where Alex lives? For example, does Alex live with another family member, with a friend, in local authority care, or somewhere else?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex lives with my ex-partner.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for explaining. Since Alex lives with your ex-partner, I have a follow-up question about that arrangement.

**Is your ex-partner already claiming Child Benefit for Alex, or are they planning to?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, my ex-partner isn't claiming Child Benefit for Alex. I do contribute towards Alex's upkeep though - I help with things like clothes and school costs.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. That's helpful to know about your contributions towards Alex.

Let me ask a bit more about the living arrangement: **Does Alex live with your ex-partner full-time, or does Alex split their time between both of your homes?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex lives with my ex-partner full-time.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. Since Alex lives full-time with your ex-partner, I need to check one more thing about Alex.

**Is Alex still in full-time education or approved training (such as A-levels, a BTEC, or an apprenticeship)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is still in sixth form doing A-levels.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information about Alex. Now I have a good understanding of Alex's situation.

Just a couple more questions about Blake (age 5) to complete my assessment:

**Do you live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. One more question:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, actually my ex-partner claims Child Benefit for Blake and Blake lives with them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. I think there may have been a small mix-up earlier - you initially mentioned that Blake lives with you, but now you've said Blake lives with your ex-partner who is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

Just to make sure I have the correct picture: **Could you confirm which child lives with you and which child lives with your ex-partner?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh, I'm sorry for the confusion. Let me clarify - Blake lives with me. Alex lives with my ex-partner, and my ex-partner is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake as well, even though Blake lives with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
No problem, thank you for clarifying that! So to summarise:

- **Blake (age 5)** lives with you, but your ex-partner is currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake
- **Alex (age 16)** lives full-time with your ex-partner and is in sixth form doing A-levels

That's helpful to understand.

Regarding Blake - since Blake lives with you but your ex-partner is claiming Child Benefit, there may be a conflict that needs to be resolved. Only one person can receive Child Benefit for each child.

**Have you and your ex-partner discussed who should claim Child Benefit for Blake, and were you unable to reach an agreement?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: We haven't really discussed it properly, no. Blake lives with me full-time, so I thought I should be able to claim for Blake.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for explaining. You're right to think that - generally, the person who the child lives with is usually considered responsible for that child and would normally be the one to receive Child Benefit.

Since Blake lives with you full-time, you would typically be the appropriate person to claim Child Benefit for Blake. However, because your ex-partner is already claiming, you would need to either:

1. Agree between yourselves that you should be the one to claim (your ex-partner would need to stop their claim and you would make a new one), or
2. If you cannot agree, HMRC (the Child Benefit Office) will decide who should receive it - and they generally prioritise the person the child lives with.

Now I have enough information to make an assessment on your eligibility. Let me provide my decision:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, here is my assessment of your eligibility for Child Benefit:

---

### **Alex (age 16)** – ❌ Not Eligible

Although Alex is under 20 and in approved education (A-levels), Alex lives full-time with your ex-partner. Under the rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for the child - meaning either they live with you, or you're contributing at least the Child Benefit amount towards their upkeep.

While you do contribute towards Alex's costs, the person the child lives with has priority for claiming. Your contributions would only allow you to *continue* receiving Child Benefit if you were already the claimant and Alex then moved elsewhere - they don't give you priority over the person Alex actually lives with. Your ex-partner would be the appropriate person to claim for Alex.

---

### **Blake (age 5)** – ✅ Eligible

Blake lives with you full-time in the UK, and you are responsible for Blake. You meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.

However, since your ex-partner is currently claiming for Blake, you'll need to resolve this:
- **Option 1:** Agree with your ex-partner that you should claim instead. They would need to stop their claim, and you would make a new one.
- **Option 2:** If you cannot agree, contact the Child Benefit Office. They will make a decision, and typically they will award Child Benefit to the person the child lives with - which is you.

---

I hope this helps clarify your situation. Is there anything else you'd like to know?

reasonings: Alex is 16 years old and in approved education (A-levels at sixth form), which means they could qualify for Child Benefit until age 20. However, Alex lives full-time with your ex-partner, not with you. According to the eligibility rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you are 'responsible' for the child - which means either the child lives with you, OR you are paying at least the same amount as the Child Benefit rate towards their upkeep. While you do contribute towards Alex's costs (clothes, school costs), the key issue is that Alex lives full-time with your ex-partner. Your ex-partner, as the person Alex lives with, would have priority for claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Your contributions would only allow you to continue receiving Child Benefit if you were already claiming and Alex then went to live elsewhere - they don't give you priority over the person the child actually lives with. Therefore, you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 5 years old and lives with you full-time in the UK. You meet the basic eligibility criteria for Child Benefit: you live in the UK and you are responsible for a child under 16 who lives with you. Although your ex-partner is currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake, only one person can receive Child Benefit for each child. Since Blake lives with you full-time, you are the person who should be receiving Child Benefit. You would need to either agree with your ex-partner that you will claim instead (they stop their claim and you make a new one), or if you cannot agree, contact the Child Benefit Office and they will decide - typically in favour of the person the child lives with. Therefore, you are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: This family has two children with different living arrangements. Alex (16) lives full-time with the ex-partner and is in approved education, so the ex-partner would be the appropriate claimant for Alex. Blake (5) lives full-time with the claimant, making the claimant eligible for Child Benefit for Blake, despite the ex-partner currently claiming. The claimant will need to resolve the duplicate claim situation for Blake with their ex-partner or through HMRC.

Blake is 5 years old and lives with you full-time in the UK. Therefore, you are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake.

response.overall_reasoning: This family has two children with different living arrangements. Alex (16) lives full-time with the ex-partner and is in approved education, so the ex-partner would be the appropriate claimant for Alex. Blake (5) lives full-time with the claimant, making the claimant eligible for Child Benefit for Blake, despite the ex-partner currently claiming. The claimant will need to resolve the duplicate claim situation for Blake with their ex-partner or through HMRC.
